podcast.name: Cross-Clamp Chronicles
podcast.blurb: Stories from the history of cardiac surgery and anesthesia — the people, the breakthroughs, and the hard-won lessons that shaped how we care for hearts today.

episodes.title: Blood Transfusion
episodes.summary: The birth of blood transfusion — from early concepts to future directions in cardiothoracic surgery.
notes: 1665 — Richard Lower performs the first documented transfusion between animals (dog to dog).
1818 — James Blundell carries out the first successful human-to-human transfusion, for postpartum hemorrhage.
1901 — Karl Landsteiner describes the ABO blood groups, explaining fatal mismatches and making transfusion safe (Nobel Prize, 1930).
1914–1916 — Sodium-citrate anticoagulation (Hustin, Agote, Lewisohn) ends the need for direct donor-to-recipient transfusion; Rous & Turner add citrate-glucose storage.
1937 — Bernard Fantus opens the first U.S. “blood bank” at Cook County Hospital, Chicago; WWII drives large-scale plasma banking (Charles Drew).
Modern era — component therapy, additive storage solutions, and patient blood management / blood conservation in the cardiac OR.
references: Giangrande PLF. The history of blood transfusion. Br J Haematol. 2000;110(4):758–767.
Learoyd P. The history of blood transfusion prior to the 20th century. Transfus Med. 2012;22(5):308–314.
Landsteiner K. Über Agglutinationserscheinungen normalen menschlichen Blutes. Wien Klin Wochenschr. 1901;14:1132–1134.
Lewisohn R. A new and greatly simplified method of blood transfusion. Med Rec. 1915;87:141–142.

resources.label: A Brief History of Blood Transfusion — Stanford Blood Center
History of Blood Transfusion — American Red Cross

episodes.title: Norman Shumway and the Birth of Heart Transplantation
episodes.summary: A brief look into the life and career of Dr. Norman Shumway — from small beginnings in southeastern Michigan to being dubbed the Father of Heart Transplantation, a titan of medical history.
notes: Norman Shumway (1923–2006), trained at the University of Minnesota, built the experimental foundation of heart transplantation at Stanford with Richard Lower.
1959–1960 — Lower & Shumway perfect orthotopic canine heart transplantation, showing a transplanted heart could resume normal function.
Dec 3, 1967 — Christiaan Barnard performs the first human heart transplant in Cape Town, using the technique pioneered by Lower & Shumway.
Jan 6, 1968 — Shumway performs the first adult heart transplant in the United States at Stanford (patient Mike Kasperak).
Early enthusiasm gave way to a near-moratorium as rejection and infection produced dismal survival.
Stanford's persistence — endomyocardial biopsy for rejection surveillance (Caves, 1973) and, later, cyclosporine — revived transplantation as durable therapy in the 1980s.
Shumway is widely remembered as the “father of heart transplantation.”
references: Lower RR, Shumway NE. Studies on orthotopic homotransplantation of the canine heart. Surg Forum. 1960;11:18–19.
Barnard CN. The operation. A human cardiac transplant: an interim report of a successful operation performed at Groote Schuur Hospital, Cape Town. S Afr Med J. 1967;41(48):1271–1274.
Caves PK, Stinson EB, Billingham ME, Shumway NE. Percutaneous transvenous endomyocardial biopsy. JAMA. 1973;225(3):288–291.
DiBardino DJ. The history and development of cardiac transplantation. Tex Heart Inst J. 1999;26(3):198–205.

resources.label: 50 Years Ago, Stanford Heart Doctors Made History — Stanford Medicine
In Memoriam: Norman E. Shumway — Texas Heart Institute Journal
